EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system tracking east across the Bering Sea at the tail end of October reached the Southwest Alaska as a new low developed along the boundary between November 1st and 2nd and strengthened up until moving ashore near the Kuskokwim Delta.  This system brought a multitude of impacts to portions of the region as it headed inland, ranging from minor coastal flooding due to strong onshore winds in portions of Bristol Bay to periods of significant falling and blowing snow, again predominantly focused near the coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tug operator moving a fuel barge reported that westerly winds produced a storm surge of 2 to 3 ft along western Kuskokwim Bay near Quinhagak overnight between 11/1 and 11/2. The combination of surge and moving ice was made it difficult to navigate that part of the bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system tracking east across the Bering Sea at the tail end of October reached the Southwest Alaska as a new low developed along the boundary between November 1st and 2nd and strengthened up until moving ashore near the Kuskokwim Delta.  This system brought a multitude of impacts to portions of the region as it headed inland, ranging from minor coastal flooding due to strong onshore winds in portions of Bristol Bay to periods of significant falling and blowing snow, again predominantly focused near the coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kipnuk Police Department reported early afternoon arrival of snow and winds with gusts up to 30 kts (35 mph) on 11/1 along with about 2 inches of new snowfall overnight.  The Kipnuk AWOS reported similar conditions during the evening of 11/1 with moderate snow and visibility down to 1/2 mile at times.  Visibility as low as 1/4 mile and moderate snow were also reported at around 6 PM AKST by the Bethel Police Department.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front associated with a low moving into Southwest Alaska pushed across the western Gulf as a new center formed at the frontal triple point and shifted east-northeast along the north Gulf Coast between November 1st and November 2nd.  Snowfall with significant variability in intensity and accumulation pushed across parts of Southcentral Alaska north of the low, with the highest amounts concentrated over the Susitna Valley and along parts of the Richardson Highway near Thompson Pass, where strong winds also caused significant blowing and drifting of snow as it fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and blowing snow were observed along portions of the Richardson Highway during the overnight of 11/1 into the morning of 11/2.  The Alaska Department of Transportation reported Road conditions through Thompson Pass as difficult to travel due to blowing and drifting snow. Low visibilities with blowing and drifting snow were reported along the Richardson Hwy from Keystone Canyon through Thompson Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving into Southwest Alaska from the Bering Sea, sending a layer of warm air aloft overtop sub-freezing surface temperatures. This resulted in a light mix of sleet, snow and freezing rain for a number of locations across the Kuskokwim Delta and Bristol Bay area.  Accumulating ice was minimal for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An employee at the NWS WSO in Bethel reported a mix of freezing rain and ice pellets falling at around 7 AM on the morning of November 30th.  Several AWOS sensors nearby also reported several instances of unknown precipitation from around the same time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system tracking east across the Bering Sea at the tail end of October reached the Southwest Alaska as a new low developed along the boundary between November 1st and 2nd and strengthened up until moving ashore near the Kuskokwim Delta.  This system brought a multitude of impacts to portions of the region as it headed inland, ranging from minor coastal flooding due to strong onshore winds in portions of Bristol Bay to periods of significant falling and blowing snow, again predominantly focused near the coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of blowing snow and snow were reported at a number of AWOS sites across mainly the western half of Bristol Bay, with the worst conditions progressing from west to east between the afternoon of November 1st into the evening and overnight hours. Dillingham Police reported that wind and snow began around Noon AKST 11/1 with visibility dropping to 1/4 mile or less at times and total snowfall of up to 4 to 6 inches per a report via Dillingham radio.  Winds Gusted pretty hard, but no damage was reported.  The Dillingham AWOS also reported 1/2 mile snow with wind gusts near 30 mph around 2 PM AKST 11/1.  Nearby AWOS sites reported similar or worse conditions as the heaviest snowfall and strongest winds moved past, including Platinum, Koliganek, Manokotak and Iguigig.  Much stronger gusts were observed at some of these sites, including a gust up to 50 kts or 57 mph at Platinum at 11 AM AKST 11/1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front associated with a low moving into Southwest Alaska pushed across the western Gulf as a new center formed at the frontal triple point and shifted east-northeast along the north Gulf Coast between November 1st and November 2nd.  Snowfall with significant variability in intensity and accumulation pushed across parts of Southcentral Alaska north of the low, with the highest amounts concentrated over the Susitna Valley and along parts of the Richardson Highway near Thompson Pass, where strong winds also caused significant blowing and drifting of snow as it fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall fell over the Susitna Valley from the evening of November 1st into November 2nd.  Reports from public and spotters for storm total snowfall were up to a foot or more, including a report of 17 inches recorded near Skwentna, 12 inches in Yentna and 8.6 inches near Amber Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak system brought in a warm nose overrunning a cold layer near the surface across Southcentral, with weak large scale lift generating periods of freezing rain/drizzle across parts of the region on November 20th.  Ice accumulations were very minor for this precipitation event, largely due to temperatures warming above freezing as the day progressed and the very light precipitation intensity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Talkeetna ASOS reported periods of light freezing rain during the afternoon of 11/20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very potent frontal system progressed across the eastern Bering on the evening of 11/6 into the morning of 11/7, sending a surge of deep, strong southerly flow into the Alaska Peninsula.  The highest wind gusts occurred at higher elevations and where strong southerly flow funneled through gaps in terrain, with peak gusts pushing above 80 mph in a couple locations.  Warm air advection was strong enough to turn most falling precipitation along the front over to rain, even farther north and east along coastal portions of Bristol Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South-southeast winds came up ahead of a strong, storm force front higher than expected in Port Heiden. Stronger winds (65-75 mph gusts) began right around Midnight AKST on the evening of 11/6 at the Port Heiden AWOS, then picked up into High Wind Warning levels by about 2:30 AM AKST.  Strong winds lasted a remarkable amount of time, with gusts hitting near/above warning criteria for about 9 hours, or from 12 AM to 9 AM AKST 11/7.  The strongest gust recorded of 86 mph occurred at 8:20 AM AKST 11/7.  Local terrain enhancement from gaps in the Aleutian Range south of Port Heiden likely played a role in this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak system brought in a warm nose overrunning a cold layer near the surface across Southcentral, with weak large scale lift generating periods of freezing rain/drizzle across parts of the region on November 20th.  Ice accumulations were very minor for this precipitation event, largely due to temperatures warming above freezing as the day progressed and the very light precipitation intensity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of light freezing rain/drizzle were reported during the early afternoon of 11/20 at both the PANC and PAED ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moving south within already strong northerly flow helped support strong, gusty winds out of gaps and passes along the north Gulf Coast near Prince William Sound between November 28th and 29th, namely through Thompson Pass.  Similarly strong winds occurred with nearly the same setup with strong northerly flow again late on the 30th into the morning on December 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On Nov 29, the highest measured wind gust was 66 mph at the PAVD ASOS.|The Thompson Pass Department of Transportation reported that winds were definitely gusting to at least 70 mph in Thompson Pass, and about 60 mph in town between the evening of November 28th and the morning of November 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak system brought in a warm nose overrunning a cold layer near the surface across Southcentral, with weak large scale lift generating periods of freezing rain/drizzle across parts of the region on November 20th.  Ice accumulations were very minor for this precipitation event, largely due to temperatures warming above freezing as the day progressed and the very light precipitation intensity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of freezing rain and snow was reported at the PAEN ASOS near midday 11/20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved east-southeast starting over the eastern Bering, then crossed the Alaska Peninsula and passed just south of Kodiak Island between November 26th and 27th.  This system and its front were able to interact favorably with much colder air in place near and north of the low as it tracked east, with deep frontal lift and seasonably high moisture contributing to efficient precipitation production and a short but intense duration of snow shifting from Bristol Bay to Kodiak Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report was received at about 12 PM November 27th estimating 9 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved east-southeast starting over the eastern Bering, then crossed the Alaska Peninsula and passed just south of Kodiak Island between November 26th and 27th.  This system and its front were able to interact favorably with much colder air in place near and north of the low as it tracked east, with deep frontal lift and seasonably high moisture contributing to efficient precipitation production and a short but intense duration of snow shifting from Bristol Bay to Kodiak Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling around 11:15 PM on Nov 27 in Kodiak City.  A spotter reported 7.5 inches of snow at 8 AM November 28th, with around 12 inches total for the snowfall event. Another report was received of 8.25 inches of snow just before 12 PM. A school bus got stuck on a side street near Kodiak City during the same morning. Many additional snowfall reports across Kodiak Island were reported during the course of the day on November 28th, with the highest amounts near 12 inches.  A car accident involving 8 vehicles also occurred where heavy snow was a contributing factor in Kodiak City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moving south within already strong northerly flow helped support strong, gusty winds out of gaps and passes along the north Gulf Coast near Prince William Sound between November 28th and 29th, namely through Thompson Pass.  Similarly strong winds occurred with nearly the same setup with strong northerly flow again late on the 30th into the morning on December 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report from the Thompson Pass Department of Transportation confirmed 70mph estimated gusts through Thompson Pass between the evening of November 30th into the morning of December 1st.  A gust to 69 mph was also recorded at the PAVD ASOS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several AWOS sensors across Bristol Bay reported periods of light freezing rain near midday on November 30th, including Dillingham and King Salmon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep area of low pressure caused strong winds in eastern Norton Sound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong easterly winds persisted over eastern Norton Sound for over 24 hours with routine over 60 mph. The strong winds caused the schools to be cancelled in Unalakleet. The winds also caused damage to sheet metal roofing of several sheds in Unalakleet and knocked over two light poles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with an Arctic airmass resulted in very cold wind chill values across the North Slope.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Point Thomson reported wind chill values colder than -60 degrees Fahrenheit for over 24 hours, with -63 degrees Fahrenheit being the coldest value reported.

CZ_NAME: NE. SLOPES OF THE ERN AK RNG
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather front brought significant snow to the eastern Alaska Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trims DOT Camp reports just 12-24 inches of snow south of the camp along the Richardson Highway in a little over 24 hours. This caused the Richardson Highway to be closed for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On October 5th, 2022 a strong area of low pressure moved over far eastern Russia, reaching the Chukchi Sea late in the evening on the 5th. The low strengthened to 955 millibars as it passed over the Wrangel Island in the early morning hours on the 6th of October. The low slowly moved to the northwest and weakened as it moved across the Chukchi Sea through the night of October 7th. A front associated with the low passed over St. Lawrence Island on the afternoon of the 5th. This front continued to push east across the Chukchi Sea the night of the 5th and Arctic Coast of Alaska on the 6th. The strongest winds were associated with this front. Winds became more southwesterly behind the front, causing water levels to rise from the Seward Peninsula to Utqiagvik. A second front moved from west to east across the Arctic Coast on the 7th, with the southwest winds shifting to become westerly behind the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water levels began rising early in the morning hours on the 6th. Water levels peaked in Point Lay at approximately 6 feet above the normal high tide line in the early morning hours on the 7th. The high water levels in the village of Point Lay caused salt water to enter the freshwater lagoon that is the source of the village's dinking water. The increased wave action caused damage to a dock in Point Lay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mature North Pacific low lifted north into the western Gulf of Alaska on December 25, as it interacted with a Bering Sea low, becoming an elongated trough in the upper levels as warm air aloft moved in. The resultant surface features became stagnant through the 27th as a slight warming and sometimes windy pattern impacted Southcentral Alaska. This lead to areas of snow, blowing snow, a wintry mix and freezing rain depending on proximity to the coast, elevation and areas of downsloping/warming winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alaska 511 reported freezing rain on the Sterling Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Bering low pushed a strong front into the Southwest Coast between December 3rd and 4th, sending strong southerly winds gusting as high as 60 to 70 mph into the coast along with a minor storm surge up to a few feet above the normal high tide line.  Strong southerly winds also made it into the northern coastline of Bristol Bay, but faulty gauge data and difficulty in getting feedback from local communities left existence of any surge or impacts unclear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surge predictions across the Kuskokwim Delta Coast were projected to be 2 and 5 feet above normal high tide with a push of strong southerly winds along a strong front pushing into Southwest.  Winds moving in with the front outperformed expectations, with gusts around 55 mph recorded at PABE and around 60-70 mph at Platinum late in the morning to early afternoon of 12/4.  Tidal gauge data from Kipnuk and Kwigillingok suggest a 2 to 3 feet surge above the normal high tide mark occurred.||An Alaska native living in one of the towns along the Kuskokwim Delta Coast verbally relayed the following to NWS Anchorage We got our boats, 4wheelers on high places, tying up things. We got the heavy wind and the high water yesterday.  Keep up the good work, and the warnings to our villages in regards to the coastal flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving northeastward through the Gulf of Alaska sent heavy precipitation in the form of heavy snow to the Copper River Basin and Prince William Sound area as it ran into Arctic air already in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Office received reports from various backcountry organizations including the Department of Transportation of 16 inches of snow overnight Wednesday into Thursday morning at the summit of the pass. Another 10 inches was reported after that by another forecaster.  Road conditions were hazardous from 700 feet to 1000 feet in elevation with water streaming down over ice and slush. Ice breaking down made the road feel like gravel and pot-holes. Plows were working hard to keep up with the snowfall rates. Visibility at the top was reduced to 1/4 to 1/2 mile at times due to heavy snow rates, resuspension from trucks, and wind.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Manokotak reported one half mile visibility or lower with moderate snow and freezing fog for 3 hours from January 20th 12:21 PM until January 20th 15:25 PM as well as winds northeasterly near 22 knots sustained with gusts to 28 knots. Manokotak, Togiak, and Platinum all reported around 3 hours at one half mile visibility or below from noon to 3 pm AKST, although Togiak winds were unknown, and Platinum had winds near 12-15 knots sustained. Manokotak experienced intermittent periods of one half mile visibility before and after the main bulk of the event from 10 am through 6 pm on the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic high pressure system set up over Southwest Alaska during the February 5-8th time frame. As a a result, there was an increased pressure gradient which created for sustained winds greater than 15 mph in conjunction with the cold which created for bitter cold wind chills to -40 degrees and lower.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bethel ASOS (PABE) recorded a wind chill of -42 degrees and sustained wind of 15 mph. Quinhagak (PAQH)- reported an air temperature of -26 degrees with a 14 knot wind and wind chill of -54 degrees on February 7th at 11:44am. On February 7th, Bethel ASOS (PABE) at the 2pm ob reported less than -40 degree wind chills and greater than 15 mph winds from 9:58 am to 1:53 pm.  |Nunapitchuk had a prolonged period of wind chills less than or equal to -40 degrees but winds remained below 15 mph.|Quinhagak recorded some of the most extreme actual temperatures as cold as -33F, but winds did not reach greater than or equal to 15 mph until later from 11:30 AM February 7th lasting until 2 pm when wind chills reached -45 to -55F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A far reaching winter storm brings high winds to the Anchorage area with snow and blowing snow for the Kuskokwim Delta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Toksook Bay reported one quarter mile visibility, heavy snow and freezing fog at approximately 7:04am on the 24th. Winds were also sustained out of the west-northwest at 26 knots with gusts of 33 knots. Peak winds of 38 knots was also reported. Public out across the Kuskokwim Delta and in Toksook Bay said very bad conditions wind blown snow, very low visibilities as low as 50 ft, 2-4ft drifts, low ceiling, winds subsiding at time of the call. Also mentioned hard to see how much snow has fallen due to the blowing and drifting. Also called the police department in Bethel and they reported really wet snow especially 6:30-8AM this morning along with freezing and glazing on the highway. Also called Hooper Bay police department and they reported blowing snow with approx 2-3 of snow with clearing skies occurring at the time of the call.

CZ_NAME: CAPE DECISION TO SALISBURY SOUND COASTAL AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river with an associated low center in the far western gulf brought a large slug of moisture into the northeast Gulf Coast staring early morning Friday into Friday evening.  With the low center well to the southwest, a wedge of colder air held firm across the Yakutat area with northeast winds, thus keeping warming at bay and allowing a very heavy, wet snow to fall.  Originally the warm air was supposed to push into the coast by around 9 AM or so, but the strength of the cold air wedge held firm until around 4 PM this afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee estimated 8 inches of snowfall since midnight at 10:30 am. Snow continued to fall until accumulations reached 16 inches of heavy wet snow from this event.

CZ_NAME: JUNEAU BOROUGH AND NORTHERN ADMIRALTY ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system in the Gulf of Alaska brings Taku winds to Juneau ahead of the storm, and widespread winds and snow across Southeast Alaska. Heavy snow transitioned to rain across much of the area. This along with antecedent snowfall created roof load issues across the are with structural collapses in Juneau and Yakutat. Rain on snowpack and frozen ground created icy conditions across the area that impacted transportation for days following the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station at AML dock in Juneau reported wind gusts up to 70 mph.

Toksook Bay reported one quarter mile visibility and heavy snow plus freezing fog for multiple hours February 1 along with gusty winds up to 50 mph creating for blizzard conditions and temperatures in the teens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moving northward from the North Pacific brought milder air into Southern Alaska resulting in mixed precipitation as well as blowing snow for some of the higher terrain. Light freezing rain continued until 6:54pm when King Salmon ASOS started to report light rain and a temperature of 34 degrees). Dillingham ASOS reported light freezing rain and light snow at 6:55pm on the 31st. Koliganek and New Stuyahok were experiencing gusty winds upwards of 48 knots and 44 knots respectively along with heavy snow fall leading to some blowing snow creating for blizzard-like conditions. Both places experienced 3 hours and more of potentially blizzard conditions though temperatures were in the upper 20s to near 30 degrees therefore, this may have limited blowing snow and might have been more snowfall intensity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure aloft shifted northeast out of the western Bering Sea on December 31, 2021, strengthening over northern Alaska January 1-3, 2022. The development of this ridge, caused an extremely cold air mass over northern Alaska to dive southward across southern Alaska and into the Gulf of Alaska. This is an ideal setup for a Bora wind event over Southcentral Alaska, whereby very cold air is forced over the Alaska Range and southward across Southcentral Alaska, leading to widespread strong and gusty winds and cold temperatures. This set-up allowed several noteworthy details to occur: |1. Very cold air, bottoming out at about -31F to -40F, moved over the Alaska Range and into the Copper River Basin at about 3kft above ground, on January 2nd. Surface temperatures dropped to single digits on the 2nd, and below zero on January 4th.|2. Amplified pressure differentiations developed between the northeast Gulf and northern Alaska. The strengthening surface low over the Gulf dropped to about 972 mb, while an extremely strong surface high topped out around 1052 mb on January 2nd. This created extremely tight pressure gradients between the northern Alaska high and the Gulf low, especially over Southcentral Alaska. This led to the development of strong winds from the northeast over the period, accelerating through the Matanuska Valley.|3. The presence of both thermal and pressure gradients combined to create much stronger winds than would otherwise be observed if standing alone.||While the region hardest hit by this high wind and cold weather event was along the Matanuska-Susitna Valley, many other locations across southcentral were also impacted by frigid temperatures and windy conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Of significance was the longevity and extremes recorded during the event. First, the strongest winds recorded were at the Palmer Airport with a peak wind gust of 88 mph at 5:36 PM January 2nd, and a gust to 91 mph at the Glenn-Parks Highway Interchange at about the same time. This rivals the strongest gusts reached at the Palmer Airport since recording began in 1972, becoming the 5th strongest wind gust recorded at the Palmer Airport, though the four strongest gusts only occurred over two separate days, December 23, 1996 and January 17, 2005, ranging between 90 and 112 mph (see graph below). The Wasilla Airport peaked at 74 mph on January 2nd at 10:16 PM.||Second, the longevity of the event was pronounced. Not only was there about 40 hours of hurricane force winds (74 mph or greater) noted at the Palmer Airport, but there were about 51 hours of wind gusts 50 mph or greater between January 1st and 3rd. Taking a regional perspective and including Anchorage in the picture, this Bora Wind event ranks 5th for longevity since records began, though is likely the strongest wind event associated with a Bora Windstorm for Palmer on record.||The final aspect of note was the extreme cold associated with the event and longevity to which the cold lingered. While temperatures dropped into the single digits by January 2nd, temperatures dropped below zero January 4th and remained frigid through January 7th, before increasing back into the teens. This proved to be quite impactful as winds took down many power lines, causing difficulties for many in the extreme cold and causing pipes and water mains to break.||We received reports of blown-over trucks, toppled Cessna airplanes at the Palmer Airport, downed trees and power lines (with power outages across the valley causing pipes to burst and people to seek shelter elsewhere due to the prolonged cold), windows broken, cars damaged due to flying debris, and siding ripped off homes and businesses. Emergency Management reported multiple CO poisoning calls, requests to be transported to the hospital, along with cases of frostbite.||Links to news coverage: https://www.adn.com/alaska-news/mat-su/2022/01/03/in-pictures-windstorm-batters-palmer-and-wasilla/, https://www.adn.com/alaska-news/weather/2022/01/02/thousands-without-power-as-heavy-winds-hit-southcentral-alaska/, https://www.alaskasnewssource.com/2022/01/01/power-outages-occurring-across-southcentral/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=snd&utm_content=ktuu, and https://www.alaskasnewssource.com/2022/01/02/strong-winds-affecting-power-travel-mat-su/?fbclid=IwAR1IQvdqJGGfv0jw85F0FUqprDwQefUzrp9dN3H_O8AelpHyu7S8mv3q4qY.||The Executive Weather Summary can be found here: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1oYQb2EPRyj5bM-rkA56RKFD-nsjs5EHs96BE24bpyMo/edit?usp=sharing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought strong winds to the coastal regions of Southeast Alaska, with some locations reporting high wind speeds.

CZ_NAME: JUNEAU BOROUGH AND NORTHERN ADMIRALTY ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Short shortwave passing across area with solid cold air advection behind and a well-defined critical level passing this evening caused high winds in the Juneau area, particularly Downtown, Douglas, and Lemon Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alaska Marine Lines dock weather station reported wind speeds up to 77 mph in this Taku Wind event. Widespread reports in the 60s and 70s occurred across downtown Juneau weather stations. Power outages and damage to an outdoor bar were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong are of high pressure over the Beaufort Sea combined with a deep area of low pressure over the Bering Strait resulted in strong easterly winds developing along the Arctic Coast. The strong winds brought blizzard conditions along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed along the Beaufort Sea coast the morning of the 15th. Nuiqsut saw gusts as high as 42 mph while Deadhorse saw gusts as high as 35 mph. The strong winds caused blowing snow to develop at both locations with several hours where the visibility below one quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong are of high pressure over the Beaufort Sea combined with a deep area of low pressure over the Bering Strait resulted in strong easterly winds developing along the Arctic Coast. The strong winds brought blizzard conditions along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Easterly winds with gusts as high as 49 mph developed in Point Lay the morning of April 15th. Blowing snow associated with the strong winds caused the visibility to drop down to less than one quarter mile for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low and front moved into the gulf from the south causing high winds over portions of the southern panhandle while the northern borders had colder northerly flow to start and snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines Customs reported heavy snow had fallen through the evening with 6.2 inches measured by 10 pm on the 23rd. By their 7 AM COOP report the next day, snow had changed to rain and compacted down to 5.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal band moved across the northern Gulf of Alaska while there was sufficient cold air in place across the northern inner channels for quickly accumulating, very wet, snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters on the Chilkat Peninsula, south of Haines, reported heavy wet snow fell during the day on the 25th. One measured 6 inches by 830pm and another measured 9 inches at 1127pm as it was changing to rain. Spotters the wet snow was causing branches to lean across the road and driveway. As the snow transitioned to rain there were strong wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeast Alaska had been impacted by a number of atmospheric rivers through September to keep streamflow on small and large rivers high. Another atmospheric river lifted out of the north Pacific as the associated warm front moved over Southeast Alaska it produced heavy rain over the northern inner channels of the panhandle. The front also advected a lot of warm air with freezing levels near 10 thousand feet. The complex terrain of the region enhance the rainfall with 1.5 to 3.5 inches of rain fell over a 12 to 18 hour period. ||The heavy rain and significant ice melt from the very warm temperatures in the headwaters of the Taiya River produced a lot of overland flow. This caused moderate flooding along the Taiya River from the early morning hours to the late afternoon on October 1st. There was road closures, damage to National Park Service infrastructure along the Chilkoot trail and near the Dyea campground. This was also the 4th highest peak flood on record. There was also moderate flooding along Jordan Creek in Juneau from the early morning to the afternoon hours on October 1st. The crested was the 2nd highest peak flood on record. A large landslide along the Haines Highway around 23 mile that came down in the overnight hours of October 1st and blocked the road. The area around the landslide reported 1 to 1.5 inches of rain in 12 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain was falling over the Chilkat Valley with 1 inch of rain falling in a 6 hour time frame. By Saturday morning on October 1st the Haines Police and Alaska DOT reported that a landslide came down during the overnight hours around 23 mile along the Haines Highway and blocked both lanes. It took all day for DOT to reopen the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large winter storm moved northward from the North Pacific setting up a tight pressure gradient across Southwest Alaska and the Eastern Aleutians. As the precipitation moved into these areas, it combined with the winds to cause reductions in visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Koliganek AWOS reported one half mile visibility with moderate snow and freezing fog with sustained winds of 35 mph and gusts to 40 mph on the 27th for much of the afternoon. Manokotak recorded similar conditions as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large winter storm moved northward from the North Pacific setting up a tight pressure gradient across Southwest Alaska and the Eastern Aleutians. As the precipitation moved into these areas, it combined with the winds to cause reductions in visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whiteout conditions with zero visibility reported via Seward Hwy Traffic Report on Facebook. Portage (ASOS) reported one quarter mile visibility with moderate snow and freezing fog on the 27th around 4:13 pm. Wind were sustained out of the east 31 mph with gusts to 55 mph. These weather conditions lasted through 9-930 PM on the 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large winter storm moved northward from the North Pacific setting up a tight pressure gradient across Southwest Alaska and the Eastern Aleutians. As the precipitation moved into these areas, it combined with the winds to cause reductions in visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kodiak reported one quarter mile visibility with moderate snow and freezing fog. Winds were also sustained out of the southeast at 24 knots with gusts to 34 knots. The event peaked on the 27th at 1:29 PM when the observation reported one quarter mile visibility with heavy snow and freezing fog. Winds were also sustained 25 knots with gusts to 35 knots out of the southeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure moving northward from the North Pacific set up a tight pressure gradient across the area in conjunction with a band of snowfall resulting in limited visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dutch Harbor ASOS (PADU) reported sustained winds of the north at 21 knots with gusts to 29 knots. One half mile visibility with moderate snow and freezing fog was also observed on the 19th at 2:34 am. The peak of the event was on the 19th throughout the day when Dutch Harbor observed sustained north winds of 30 knots with gusts to 35 knots. Also, one quarter mile visibility, heavy snow and freezing fog was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving northward through the Gulf of Alaska sent precipitation into the Arctic air already in place across the Copper River Basin. As a result, a heavy snowfall occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alaska 511 at the Richardson highway mile post 148 to mile post 184 reported very difficult road conditions, blowing and drifting snow, ice glaze, icy patches, packed snow, rough icy patches, snow on roadway, visibility reduced, resuspended snow as well. This was on the March 10th 6 AM Report. Very difficult road conditions reported on 511 website for the March 10th 6 AM update along the Richardson Highway north of Gakona. 6 to 12 inches of new snowfall overnight reported in conditions update.  DOT Calls on the 10th at 1 PM to report the following:  Tok Cutoff - 6 inches, light snow still falling, Richardson Highway mile post 115-147 - 8 inches, light snow still falling, Richardson Highway mile post 148 thru Paxson - 10 inches, light snow still falling.  Roads hazardous.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing low tracked northward along a preexisting front and into the SE gulf on the evening of 3/29. The low center stayed near the coast of Prince of Wales Island, causing strongest winds through Clarence Strait.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated weather observing station PAMM in Metlakatla measured 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river moved out of the north Pacific into the Gulf of Alaska September 25th and started to impact the northern half of the Southeast Alaska's panhandle. This event brought in very high amounts of moisture, winds and very warm temps through September 27th. These warm temps rose the freezing levels to be above 9 thousand feet to increase the amount of melt from glacier in headwaters of the Taiya River. Six hour rainfall amounts ranged from 0.50 to as much as 1.75 at higher elevation. This is after the area had been experiencing heavy rainfall.

This event brought in very high amounts of moisture, winds and very warm temps through September 27th. These warm temps rose the freezing levels to be above 9 thousand feet to increase the amount of melt from glacier in headwaters of the Taiya River. Rain amounts from this event ranged from 3 to 5 inches in 36 hours. The heavy rain and high freezing levels produced significant amounts of runoff to cause moderate flood along the Taiya River near Skagway in Dyea and around the Jordan Creek area near Juneau from mid morning on September 26th through the morning of September 27th. ||As the front moved through the area there was a wind shift in the afternoon hours of September 26th and with the ground very wet from the heavy rain caused a tree to fall which triggered a shallow landslide in downtown Juneau to damaged 3 homes. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 1700AKST on September 26th there was a strong wind shift to the west after hours of strong southeast winds and heavy rain with 3 hour amounts of over 0.50 leading up to the event. The ground was saturated and the wind shift triggered a large tree to fall from steep terrain in downtown Juneau to produce a shallow landslide. The material hit 3 homes with one resting on the one below it. There was no one was injured but there was significant damage to the 3 homes that were impacted. The impact area was isolated with other residences in the area evacuated until the area was declared safe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought strong winds to the coastal regions of Southeast Alaska, with some locations reporting high wind speeds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ketchikan Airport reported a wind gust of 58 mph.

AKDOT was able to clear the material in a few hours to have the road back open.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure built over interior Southwest Alaska for June 26th. At the surface was a decent pressure gradient resulting in windy conditions in combination with hot and dry conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Historically dry fuels prompted the issuance of this Red Flag Warning knowing that criteria would all likely not be met.  Given high fire activity in the area and large fire spread, this was verified on impacts based criteria in coordination with the IMET.  The fire grew up to 3 miles on many fronts, but they knew what to expect and took appropriate action/safety measures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Strong area of high pressure over the Beaufort Sea brought strong winds to the eastern Arctic Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions occurred at both Barter Island and Point Thomson. The strongest winds occurred on Barter Island where winds gusted as high as 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled weather front brought significant snow to the southeast Interior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer east of Delta Junction reported 12 inches of snow with this event. The snow began on the evening of the 9th and tapered off the evening of the 10th. West of Delta Junction public reports ranged from 4 to 6 inches of accumulation with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled weather front brought significant snow to the southeast Interior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in Northway just before 11 PM on the 8th, and by 11 PM on the 9th 8 inches of snow had fallen. In Tok, 9.7 inches of snow was measured by the COOP observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of high pressure over the Beaufort Sea combined with an area of low pressure over southern Alaska caused strong easterly wind that resulted in blizzard conditions over the Beaufort Sea Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Deadhorse airport reported gusts to 35 mph with visibility less than one quarter mile for just over three hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of high pressure over the Beaufort Sea combined with an area of low pressure over southern Alaska caused strong easterly wind that resulted in blizzard conditions over the Beaufort Sea Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Barter Island AWOS reported gusts as high as 45 mph with visibility as low as a quarter mile for just over three hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent, strong area of high pressure over the Arctic Ocean resulted in persistent blizzard conditions along the Chukchi Sea Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent ground blizzard occurred at Pont Lay. Pont Lay reported consistent sustained winds in excess of 35 mph with visibility as low as a quarter mile for the better part of three days. Gusts as high as 50 mph were reported during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure over the Artic combined with a strong low in the Bering Sea brought high winds to Norton Sound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed in Golovin the morning of the 10th. Wind gusts at high as 75 mph occurred during the event, with the peak around 9 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure moving northward from the North Pacific set up a tight pressure gradient across the area in conjunction with a band of snowfall resulting in limited visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold Bay ASOS (PACD) reported one quarter mile visibility with heavy snow and freezing fog. Winds were also sustained out of the northwest at 25 knots with gusts to 35 knots. Cold Bay was down between one quarter to one half mile throughout the day along with winds gusting as high as 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure moving northward from the North Pacific set up a tight pressure gradient across the area in conjunction with a band of snowfall resulting in limited visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kodiak ASOS (PADQ) reported sustained winds out of the northwest of 15 knots with gusts to 23 knots along with one half mile visibility, moderate snow and freezing fog on the 18th at 12:53 PM. Moderate to heavy snow bands moved onshore from the Gulf of Alaska bringing one half mile visibility along with gusty northwest winds to 35 mph the afternoon and evening on the 18th. Peak of the event occurred on the afternoon of the 18th when one quarter mile visibility, heavy snow, and sustained northwest winds of 21 knots with gusts to 30 knots were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure moving northward from the North Pacific set up a tight pressure gradient across the area in conjunction with a band of snowfall resulting in limited visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: False Pass reported one quarter mile visibility along with heavy snow and freezing fog. Winds were also sustained out the north-northwest at 22 knots with gusts to 29 knots. This product was issued for Cold Bay westward mostly due to the observations at False Pass airport. False Pass was down at a quarter mile or less for approximately 12 straight hours on the 19th. (between 9 AM and 9 PM).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into the Northeast Gulf of Alaska from the south with an associated front lifting northward across the Inner Channels of the Alaskan Panhandle on March 21st. An entrenched cold air mass clung to Upper Lynn Canal early on to support widespread moderate to heavy snow across the region through the 22nd of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines Customs Cooperative Observer reported 10 inches of snow in a 24 hour period, with most of it falling overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into the Northeast Gulf of Alaska from the south with an associated front lifting northward across the Inner Channels of the Alaskan Panhandle on March 21st. An entrenched cold air mass clung to Upper Lynn Canal early on to support widespread moderate to heavy snow across the region through the 22nd of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 11.8 inches of new snow at White Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold Temperatures in Lynn Canal along with moisture and lift from a passing storm system allowed for quick accumulations of snow overnight along the Chilkat Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Trained Spotter Reported 7 inches of snow near Haines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving northward into Bristol Bay area brought some warmer air northward which overran cold air at the surface. This resulted in a mix of precipitation across the Kuskokwim Delta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chevak AWOS reported sustained north-northeast winds of 27 knots with one quarter mile visibility and moderate snow on March 9th around 9-10 AM. Bethel ASOS had a period of light freezing rain that started 10 AM 3/10 shortly after the advisory was|allowed to expire. Otherwise Bethel saw almost all RA with temps hovering around 33-34 for most of the period advisory was in effect. Bethel Department of Transportation confirmed there was light freezing rain after 10 AM 3/10, but that everything fell as rain prior to then.  Also indicated temps were running a couple degrees warmer in town than the airport. Kipnuk AWOS/ASOS precipitation determination was not working (undecoded remarks PWINO FZRANO) for duration of event, though there was a period from 3z-10z 3/10 of accumulating precipitation at 32F. Kipnuk dept of public safety reported mostly rain and slush with no indication of ice accretion. Interestingly enough, the main event hitting advisory and even borderline blizzard warning criteria|occurred with one quarter mile to one half mile visibility and 25 to 30kt winds between Chevak and Hooper Bay.  The worst conditions peaked around midday 3/9 at Hooper Bay as a localized blizzard affected the area when the primary concern originally had been the freezing rain threat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved northward through the Gulf of Alaska and stalled in Prince William Sound. This resulted in a band of snow that moved over the Chugach Mountains which then stalled and intensified over Anchorage and the Matanuska Valley. As a result, significant snowfall occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local storm report at Big Lake: 9.0 inches of snow as of 7:30 PM AST 3/5/2022.

Atmospheric Conditions: Heavy snow, Poor visibility, Snow, Visibility reduced, Whiteout. Snow: 10 inches; Wind speed (MPH): Min 10, Max 40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient along with some light snow across the area resulted in snow and blowing snow which reduced visibilities across the Kuskokwim Delta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Webcams across the Kuskokwim Delta showed pretty bad conditions with reduced visibilities and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge built over Southwest Alaska June 8th and remained over the area through the 10th. At the surface, a relatively tight pressure gradient to some gusty winds in combination with the hot and dry conditions. A few upper level shortwaves moving through the flow aloft helped to aid in lift across the area. Also, daytime heating helped to destabilize the atmosphere leading to increased instability along with some moisture leading to thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong ridge of high pressure set up over Southcentral for May 30th and 31st resulting in another period of hot, dry, and windy conditions across Anchorage and the Matanuska Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a result, Red Flag Warnings were issued. Palmer verified on the 30th with temperatures in the low to mid 70s, RH values 25% or less even into the teens, and sustained winds of 15-20 mph with gusts up to 30 mph for 3 consecutive hours. Anchorage verified on the 31st at Ted Stevens International Airport with temperatures in the upper 60s to near 70 degrees, RH values of 25% or less, and sustained winds of 15 mph or more for 3 consecutive hours.

PASL-Sleetmute and PANI-Aniak: max temps 70F; min RH 21 to 30%; winds N 5 to 10 mph (N 8 to 12 w/ gusts to 18 mph in PANI). Conditions lasted from approx 10/2330Z-11/0400Z-10/3:30pm to 10/8pm AKDT.

CZ_NAME: WRN TANANA VLY WRN YUKON VLY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam formed on the Tanana River downstream of Manley Hot Springs and caused significant flooding along the Tanana River upstream of the ice jam for approximately 15 miles. Although the temperatures preceding the ice jam were normal to below normal, the Nenana Ice Classic personnel measured an ice thickness of 30���, 73% of average, on April 8th at the Tanana River at Nenana. This site is 53 miles southeast of Manley Hot Springs, but it is a reasonable indicator of basin-wide conditions. The Natural Resources Conservation Services snow water equivalent (SWE) data reported many stations with a three record snowpack in the upper Tanana River basin with an estimated basin-wide SWE more than 200% of normal. It is likely that the robust snowpack contributed to the flooding due to the large surge of water moving downriver past Manley Hot Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water levels rose to bankfull in Manley Hot Springs the evening of May 6th and by the morning of May 7th major flooding was observed in the community. NWS staff on a Civil Air Patrol reconnaissance flight observed a large ice jam on the Tanana River 12 miles downriver which dammed the river and backed up water into the community. The river crested around 9 am May 8th with a preliminary estimated stage of 19.50 feet, which is 6.0 feet above flood stage. This was the highest water level observed at Manley Hot Springs since the record flood of 1956 (the 1956 flood is estimated approximately 2.5 feet higher). The ice jam held for 36 hrs, releasing on the morning of May 8th and water levels began to recede through the day. Standing water remained in low lying areas of the community through the following week. Impacts to the community included many homes and structures either flooded or surrounded by water, many roads inundated with some parts of town appearing cut off and only accessible by boat, Only 6 of freeboard under the Manley Hot Springs Slough bridge, approximately 500 feet of the Elliot Highway approaching town was inundated with several feet of water, power was shut down in the community as a precaution due to the lines being buried, cellular, phone, and internet networks are down. In addition significant areas of fuel sheen on the water surface due to overturned heating fuel tanks and other sources.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam formed at Sand Island, adjacent to McGrath, at 5pm on May 10th and  held for 26 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam formed at Sand Island, adjacent to McGrath, at 5pm on May 10th . Before the ice jam, waterleaves were near bankfull, and the ice jam caused the river to rise 4.5 feet in 6 hours ��� from 19.2��� at 5 PMto 23.7��� and 11PM. The ice jam held for 26 hours, releasing at 6pm on 5/11, with the water dropping rapidly. The flooding caused damage to Chinana Avenue, the main road in town as well as the road to the dump. The flood damaged the sewage lagoon and the dump. Water entered basements and crawlspaces of around 15 homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge set up over much of Southwest Alaska during this time frame. At the surface, a relatively tight pressure gradient resulted in gusty winds along with the hot and dry conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Red Flag Warning conditions were met for T/RH at STNA2/Stoney RAWS 2pm to 8pm 6/30. Winds did not hit technical criteria at this site, but winds across the zone for higher elevation sites were gusting in the 30 to 50 mph range nearly continuously for much of 6/30 afternoon and overnight.  |(Example: Kilae Creek N18KX, elevation 1877 ft).  Fire behavior was notably active on GOES/POES imagery during the afternoon and evening of 6/30 across both 163/153 zones.||Extremely dry fuel conditions prompted issuance despite explict Red Flag Warning|conditions not expected to be met.  Strong southeasterlies with upper 70s temps had a significant impact to larger fires, with notably active fire activity and a very large, dense smoke plumes visible on POES/GOES imagery PM 6/30 over both 153/163 zones.||PANI- Aniak/PALG- Kalskag also ended up impacted by extremely dense smoke spreading NW off of the Lime Complex fires, with both sites falling to 1/4SM or less from around 8pm 6/30 to 2am 7/1.  Dense Smoke Advisories were issued for zones 152- Lower Kuskokwim Valley/155- Kuskokwim Delta in accordance with the low visibility smoke observed by both AWOS/ASOS and obvious support from webcams ||Notes from an IMET (on 7/2) for overall Hot, Dry, Windy conditions/fire reactions and 153/163 Red Flag Warnings: The first day we had the Red Flags out, the fires really responded and we definitely verified them from a fire perspective...If I were to redo yesterday, I probably would've kept the Red Flag for 163 but not 153 (the western fires did not see much activity. With the historically dry fuels we were worried about the winds even though we knew it wouldn't hit the Temp or RH criteria). But, hindsight is 20/20 and they appreciated the advance notice of this event, because as a whole the fires grew a lot. The Ops chief did mention seeing Red Flag level activity on the eastern fires yesterday though, so the 163 red flag warning certainly verified.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level ridge of high pressure developed over much of Southcentral Alaska for May 20-22nd resulting in hot, dry, and windy conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures in the upper 60s to low 70s, low min RH values, as well as windy conditions occurred between May 20th-22nd. This resulted in Red Flag Warning conditions for this time frame. At the time we were still in the pre-greenup conditions phase meaning temperatures greater than or equal to 65 degrees, min RH less than 25%, and sustained winds greater than or equal to 15 mph, RAWS sites greater than or equal to 11 mph. Conditions were met at PANC- Ted Stevens International Airport for 3 or more hours, Palmer Airport in the Matanuska Valley, Kenai and Soldotna on the Kenai Peninsula.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong upper level ridge set up over the southern Alaska Mainland resulting in hot, dry, and windy conditions for the May 26th-27th time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to still being in the pre-greenup phase, Red Flag Warnings were issued for the Anchorage Bowl, Matanuska Valley, Kenai Peninsula, Susitna Valley, and Bristol Bay. Glen Alps in the Anchorage verified with 65 degrees or higher, RH 25% or less, and sustained winds of 11 mph or greater for 3 hours. Palmer verified in the Matanuska Valley reporting 65 degrees or higher, RH 25% or less and sustained winds of 15 mph or more for 3 hours. Soldotna on the Kenai Peninsula verified with temperatures in the low 70s, RH values 25% or less and sustained winds around 15 mph for 3 hours. Talkeetna in the Susitna Valley verified with temperatures in the low 70s, RH values 25% or less even into the teens, and sustained winds of 15 mph or more for 3 hours. Koliganek in Bristol Bay observed temperatures around 70 degrees, RH values 25% or less, and sustained winds of 15 mph or more for 3 consecutive hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple systems moving through Southcentral with rounds of heavy rain with not much dry time in between them resulted in rivers on the Kenai Peninsula especially the Kenai River remaining at elevated water levels through this time frame.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: River gage on the Kenai River remained in action stage for over a week due to the persistent rainfall from day to day. Low lying areas of a campground nearby reported higher than usual water levels during the timeframe of the 18th-27th but only minor flooding reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt flooding in the Copper River Basin, specifically along Moose Creek near the town of Glennallen, AK.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt flooding in the Copper River Basin, specifically Moose Creek near the town of Glennallen, AK, off the upper Moose Creek drainage.  Flooding was reported at the Fire Station (2-4' of water), at the AT&T equipment building, the old DOT buildings, and the Library parking lot and the Legislative Information Office were sandbagged. Flood waters were also noted at the Bureau of Land Management parking areas and roadways, surrounding some of the cabins, and blocking the roads. Pictures and reports showed the river had high, swift, dangerous waters. The Gulkana River flooded as well. Newspaper article located here: https://www.countryjournal2020.com/2022/05/moose-creek-throws-tantrum-floods-lower.html.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge built into Southcentral on June 3-4th. Some weak upper level shortwaves moved through the overall flow aloft helping to create the lift needed to support thunderstorm development. Ample daytime heating underneath the ridge led to instability with generally limited moisture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Webcams around the Susitna Valley showed thunderstorms across the region. Also, the AICC lightning map showed multiple lightning strikes in the Susitna Valley. Temperatures across the area ranged from the upper 70s to low 80s with relative humidity's in the upper teens to low 20s. Winds were also sustained between 10 to 15 mph for about an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late October system initiated an overrunning snow event for parts of the far N Panhandle as warmer air overran cooler air. Some snow occurred near surface level but the majority of it fell above 2000 feet along the Klondike Highway and near White Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 730 am AKDT, the Klondike Highway Road Report from the Department of Transportation reported a maximum of 6 inches of new snow at the summit. The next day, the Road Report noted an additional max 8 inches of new snow since the morning of October 26. Snow continued at White Pass through October 26 and the Klondike Highway was closed between 552 am AKDT and 914 am AKDT on October 27 from Skagway to Fraser due to large amounts of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The set up was a broad longwave trough in place over much of the Bering Sea through southern Alaska. Embedded in this broad longwave trough was an upper level low meandering over the Yukon-Kuskokwim Delta. Multiple upper level shortwaves rotated around this upper level low along with a jet stream aimed right up Cook Inlet into Southcentral. The southwesterly flow at 700 millibars was also very moist with all this moisture being advected northeastward into the Cook Inlet region and Mat-SU Valleys. As a result, heavy rain occurred throughout the region resulting in flooding for some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was verified by Alaska State Troopers in Wasilla. The Regional Operations Center and the State Emergency Operations Center reported on August 8th, 2022 at approximately 22:09 hours, Alaska State Troopers (AST) began receiving multiple 911 calls reporting flooding in the area of Schrock Road and Sitze Road in Wasilla.  AST responded to the area discovering the road has been washed out and is impassible just past the Little Susitna River Bridge due to a culvert failing.  Matsu Borough along with DOT is on scene working to repair the culvert and roadway.  It���s also been advised there is a bridge farther down Sitze Road that may be compromised and is being assessed.  At this time, residence past this washout are un accessible.  Additional patrols of the area also discovered North Sushana Drive is impassible due to the Little Susitna River flooding the road.  These neighborhoods are also un accessible at this time. There were pictured posted on social media from the Alaska State Department of Transportation. SEOC also forwarded maps & pics of flooding in Houston. A news article with pictures can be seen here: https://www.adn.com/alaska-news/mat-su/2022/08/09/mat-su-flooding-closes-roads-threatens-homes-after-deluge-soaks-talkeetna-mountains/.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The set up was a broad longwave trough in place over much of the Bering Sea through southern Alaska. Embedded in this broad longwave trough was an upper level low meandering over the Yukon-Kuskokwim Delta. Multiple upper level shortwaves rotated around this upper level low along with a jet stream aimed right up Cook Inlet into Southcentral. The southwesterly flow at 700 millibars was also very moist with all this moisture being advected northeastward into the Cook Inlet region and Mat-SU Valleys. As a result, heavy rain occurred throughout the region resulting in flooding for some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Anchorage Daily News published a story about the flooding in Houston from heavy rain that fell over the Talkeetna Mountains August 8.  Numerous roads were closed in the Houston, AK township and up to 20 properties were affected. Flooding in the town of Houston and surrounding roads was widespread.   https://www.adn.com/alaska-news/mat-su/2022/08/09/mat-su-flooding-closes-roads-threatens-homes-after-deluge-soaks-talkeetna-mountains/  Flooding on the Little Susitna River at Houston reached a crest of 12.52 ft on August 10, 2022 at 5PM AKDT which was moderate flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of showers were rotating around a low in the eastern Gulf of Alaska. A very strong shower remained in place over the Juneau airport area including the drainage basin of Jordan Creek for 2 hours. The very heavy rainfall was a 50 year return period and produced significant amount of runoff to cause Jordan Creek to rapidly rise out of its banks to produce moderate flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showers were moving across the Juneau area with most of them being light to moderate. A very strong shower moved over the Juneau airport around 1500AKST and persisted for the next 2 hours. This produced 1.03 of rainfall which is a 50 year return period rainfall intensity. Jordan Creek, located very close to the airport, was already near bankfull stage of 9.2 feet from the previous showers moving over the area through the day. The creek began to rise rapidly from the heavy rain and went over minor flood stage of 9.7 feet around 1700AKST and crest at 10.73 feet above moderate flood stage of 10.50 feet by 2100AKST. There was flooding of roads, yards along with some water getting into homes near Jordan Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving eastward across the northern Gulf spread heavy rain northward into the eastern Prince William Sound area resulting in heavy rain for places such as Cordova and Valdez. As a result, numerous rivers and streams water levels rose due to the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The service hydrologist spoke to Scott Benda, the Valdez Flood Manager, and found out that rivers in the area were high with some low areas taking on water along Lowe River and Glacier Steam.  The Town of Valdez did not experience any high water or low areas of flooding.  Alpine Woods has a fortified dike, which was relined with rocks; the sub-division was not impacted by this event because of the work on the dike.  Overall all streams were high, with ample groundwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river moved out of the north Pacific into the Gulf of Alaska September 25th and started to impact the northern half of the Southeast Alaska's panhandle. This event brought in very high amounts of moisture, winds and very warm temps through September 27th. These warm temps rose the freezing levels to be above 9 thousand feet to increase the amount of melt from glacier in headwaters of the Taiya River. Rain amounts from this event ranged from 3 to 5 inches in 36 hours. The heavy rain and high freezing levels produced significant amounts of runoff to cause moderate flood along the Taiya River near Skagway in Dyea and around the Jordan Creek area near Juneau from mid morning on September 26th through the morning of September 27th. ||As the front moved through the area there was a wind shift in the afternoon hours of September 26th and with the ground very wet from the heavy rain caused a tree to fall which triggered a shallow landslide in downtown Juneau to damaged 3 homes. The heavy rain also triggered a landslide near mile marker 26 along the Haines Highway Monday afternoon and 2 near mile marker 31 along Glacier Highway in Juneau one around 1700AKST and then again in the early morning of September 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Taiya River began to rise from the heavy rain and ice melt in the early evening of September 25th with it going above minor flood stage of 16.5 feet by the early morning of September 26th. As the heavy rain persisted along with enhanced runoff from the warm temperatures��in the headwaters the Taiya River rose rapidly right through the moderate flood stage of 17 feet and crested 18.84 feet in the afternoon hours of September 26th. The heavy rain moved passed the area in the evening and the river started to recede. The Taiya River went below moderate flooding by mid morning on September 27th and below minor flood stage in the early afternoon. The National Park Service closed the Chilkoot trail due to flooding impacts of thigh deep or higher swift cold water. There was also a significant��amount of flooding that closed the Dyea campground along with a house in Dyea that was surrounded by 2 feet of water. There was some damage from erosion��to the roads in Dyea, one in particular leading to the cemetery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam which formed on the Yukon River 7 miles upstream of Grayling on May 15th resulted in Yukon River floodwaters draining into the Innoko River basin. The influx of water caused the Innoko River to rise overbank and cause minor flooding in the community of Shageluk.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water levels on the Innoko River began rising to bankfull on Friday May 20th as floodwaters from an ice jam on the Yukon River started flowing into the Innoko River upstream of Shageluk. Water levels rose overbank in Shageluk on Saturday the 21st as the surge of water moved down the Innoko River, eventually cresting on Tuesday May 24th. Water levels in Shageluk remained above flood stage through May 30th with bankfull conditions persisting into the first week of June. Residents reported water over some roads near the river, including the road to the airport which was impassable and caused the airport to only be accessible by boat. The high water also caused significant erosion of some riverbanks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam formed in the Yukon River approximately 6 miles down river of Circle causing flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam formed the evening of May 9th in that Yukon River which caused flooding in the village of Circle. This flooding caused the road to the airport to the airport to become impassable. The Steese Highway was flooded  with 12 inches of water. The high water levels caused the community well to flood requiring the well to be disinfected before it could be used again. One home had water enter the bottom floor.

These warm temps rose the freezing levels to be above 9 thousand feet to increase the amount of melt from glacier in headwaters of the Taiya River. Rain amounts from this event ranged from 3 to 5 inches in 36 hours. The heavy rain and high freezing levels produced significant amounts of runoff to cause moderate flood along the Taiya River near Skagway in Dyea and around the Jordan Creek area near Juneau from mid morning on September 26th through the morning of September 27th. ||As the front moved through the area there was a wind shift in the afternoon hours of September 26th and with the ground very wet from the heavy rain caused a tree to fall which triggered a shallow landslide in downtown Juneau to damaged 3 homes. The heavy rain also triggered a landslide near mile marker 26 along the Haines Highway Monday afternoon and 2 near mile marker 31 along Glacier Highway in Juneau one around 1700AKST and then again in the early morning of September 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jordan Creek in Juneau started to rise in response to the heavy rain through September 26th with it going over minor flood stage of 9.7 feet in the late evening hours. Water levels continued to rise at a fast pace from 12 hour rain of amounts of over 1.50 inches. The runoff from the heavy rain pushed Jordan Creek just above moderate flood stage and crested at 10.58 feet before receding below flood stage by the morning hours of September 27th. There was significant flooding of roads and parking lots around the Jordan Creek area and along Jordan Avenue. Some homes did put up sandbags to stop flood waters from coming into their homes with some water getting right up to other residences.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river front brought periods of heavy rain to the northern panhandle starting Tuesday evening August 16th, particularly at elevation. There was also significant warm temperature over the area that increased the amount of ice melt from glaciers in the Taiya River basin. A frontal wave along enhanced rainfall rates again Wednesday morning August 17th. Rainfall amounts in the headwaters of the Taiya River ranged from 1.50 to 2 inches for the event. The Taiya River went over moderate flood stage and flooded the lower portions of the Chilkoot trail with cold and swift water up to knee deep in places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The atmospheric river produced 1 to 2 inches fell over 24hrs with highest rainfall amounts at higher elevations. In addition to the rainfall there was significant snow and ice melt from very warm temps in the headwaters of the Taiya River basin and the main tributary of the Norse River basin. Rain moved into the area the night of 16th and increased through the early morning hours of the 17th and persisted into the afternoon before the rainfall moved east of the area by the late afternoon on the 17th.||The Taiya River started to rise from the moderate to heavy rain in the evening of August 16th and went over minor flood stage of 16.5 feet mid morning of August 17th and crested at 17.58 feet around 330pm which is above moderate flood stage of 17.0 feet. The river did not drop below minor flood stage until the early morning hours of August 18th. The Klondike NHP closed the Chilkoot trail to all use on August 17th. The flooding had submerged sections of the Chilkoot Trail with hazardous, swift-flowing, waist-deep water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After the two months of dry and sunny weather due to mostly upper level ridging in place across much of Alaska, the pattern flipped for the second half of the month to more of an upper level trough as the upper level ridge shifted to the east. This resulted in multiple storm systems moving into Southcentral with rounds of heavy rainfall that resulted in small streams and river rises in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Little Susitna River below Hatcher Pass Bridge peaked in action stage and was close to minor flood stage, as seen in hydrograph and data table. Data from this station's hydrograph were used to populate the verifying & peak times for this event.|Matanuska River at Old Glenn Hwy approached action stage, as seen in hydrograph and data table.|One of the emergency managers at Mat-SU EM had video of a flooded driveway but noted no other impacts had been observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure moving through the Bering brought increased surf to the Bristol Bay coastline. A tight pressure gradient due to the low brought gusty winds and helped to push the water towards the coastline raising the water levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report came in from a resident in Twin Hills. They drove 3 miles along the beach to give us an update. She noted that wave debris along gravel never made it past where the green begins.  There were areas of the beach where the sand/gravel had been washed out, making it impassable due to the larger boulders.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: APRFC tweeted pictures of Campbell Creek overtopping bank and an inundated bike path near Dimond Blvd. |Hit minor flood stage: SF Campbell Creek (data from hydrograph used for verification timing), Chester Creek|Hit action stage: Ship Creek, Campbell Creek at Dimond, Glacier Creek in Girdwood.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hydrographs ands tabular data for Willow Creek showed action stage was exceeded by |July 19 at 4pm, minor flood stage was exceeded by July 19 at 9pm, crest occurred on July 20 at 5am.|Other creeks in 145 that exceeded action stage: Little Susitna River, Willow Creek near Willow, Susitna River at Gold Creek & Parks Hwy Bridge, Talkeetna River near Railroad Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeast Alaska had been impacted by a number of atmospheric rivers through September to keep streamflow on small and large rivers high. Another atmospheric river lifted out of the north Pacific as the associated warm front moved over Southeast Alaska it produced heavy rain over the northern inner channels of the panhandle. The front also advected a lot of warm air with freezing levels near 10 thousand feet. The complex terrain of the region enhance the rainfall with 1.5 to 3.5 inches of rain fell over a 12 to 18 hour period. ||The heavy rain and significant ice melt from the very warm temperatures in the headwaters of the Taiya River produced a lot of overland flow. This caused moderate flooding along the Taiya River from the early morning hours to the late afternoon on October 1st. There was road closures, damage to National Park Service infrastructure along the Chilkoot trail and near the Dyea campground. This was also the 4th highest peak flood on record. There was also moderate flooding along Jordan Creek in Juneau from the early morning to the afternoon hours on October 1st. The crested was the 2nd highest peak flood on record. A large landslide along the Haines Highway around 23 mile that came down in the overnight hours of October 1st and blocked the road. The area around the landslide reported 1 to 1.5 inches of rain in 12 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The water levels along the Taiya River were receding from a previous rain event that produced some minor flooding on September 29th and was near to just below bankful levels by the afternoon hours of September 30th. Moderate to heavy rain along with very warm temperatures associated with a strong atmospheric river moved over the northern inner channels of Southeast Alaska through the overnight hours going into October. The strong low level winds along with abundant sub-tropical moisture being transported over the complex terrain of the region produced very high rain amounts with 1.5 to 2 inches falling in 18 hours across the headwaters of the Taiya River basin. The combination of the heavy precipitation along with ice melt from the glaciers in the headwaters caused significant amount of runoff into the Taiya River. The Taiya River rose rapidly through the overnight hours and was above moderate flood stage of 17 feet right after midnight on October 1st. The river continue to rise at a rapid rate and crested in the mid morning at 19.38 feet. This was the third highest crest at the time. There was significant amount of flooding along the Chilkoot trail and in the town of Dyea. The National Park Service campground in Dyea was closed from flood waters inundating the entire area along with water surrounding the NPS ranger station. There was significant amount of flooding of the roads going to the Dyea historic site from culverts being overwhelmed, Dyea Road near the McDermott Road turnoff had at least 6 inches of water over the road which is just past the Taiya River bridge. There was a lot of damage to NPS infrastructure along the Chilkoot trail, Dyea campground and roads around Dyea. This was one of the most impactful flooding events that the areas has seen in almost 20 years.

CZ_NAME: NE. SLOPES OF THE ERN AK RNG
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope flow combined with a very moist airmass caused training thunderstorms to form along the Alaska Range. The rainfall associated with these thunderstorms caused significant rises to small creeks draining the Alaska Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 3 inches of rain caused significant rises in creeks draining the Alaska Range from the evening of July 10th. This caused minor damage to several bridges, specifically the ones over Boulder Creek, Lower Suzy Q Creek, Upper Suzy Q Creek, Falls Creek, Gunnysack Creek, and Darling Creek. The Bridge over Bear Creek received significant damage, the approach road being washed out completely. The Richardson highway was closed between MP 281 and MP 234 for over 48 hours. Flooding also damaged a fiber optic cable which resulted in communications disruptions across the Interior.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ex-Typhoon Kulap's earlier impacts to the western Aleutians at the end of September were followed by more minor impacts to portions of the Southwest coast as the remnant low tracked very slowly east across the Bering Sea.  High waves and elevated water levels were observed along parts of the Kuskokwim Delta Coast as the filling low pressure drifted near the Southwest coastline before the system was absorbed by another low pressure system moving into the Bering by October 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tidal gauge at Kwigillingok along the Kuskokwim Delta Coast showed observed values peaking about 2 feet above the normal high tide (highest astronomical tide) line on the morning of October 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low and associated frontal band moved across the northern panhandle after a cold break, causing snow. A brief period of enhanced snow fall rates of 1 to 2 inches an hour early Thursday morning was followed by a mix with with rain through the afternoon for the Icy Strait area. The northern inner channels saw snow last longer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter and COOP in Gustavus reported over 5 inches of snow by 7am Thursday, then changed to rain in the afternoon. Measurements at Glacier Bay National Park were much higher, estimating 9-11 inches by 10:15am. Snow continued to fall into the afternoon, but 2 additional inches compacted and did not add to the depth.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell over Haines for about 24 hours with a range of accumulations of around 12 inches measured in town to 15 inches along the Chilkat Peninsula. Meanwhile, the border and out the road only had light accumulations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP in Elfin Cove measured 7.5 inches of snow by 3pm on the 10th. Similarly, the COOP observer in Pelican measured 4.5 inches by 8am on the 10th and another 3.9 inches after that.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low moved into the Bering Sea, and its associated front moved across Interior Alaska bringing very strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed over eastern Norton Sound. Shaktoolik had gusts to 61 mph and Unalakleet had gusts to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air was in place as a front moved in from the west bringing in warmer air and moisture. White Pass saw accumulating snow, but the upper Chilkat Valley was the big winner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines Customs reported 13.3 inches of snow by 7am on the 3rd and snow had ended, but melted down to 0.52 inches of liquid. Spotter reports from nearby said that light snow began around 1pm on the 2nd and increased through the afternoon with occasional strong gusts. Closer to town snow changed to rain overnight with just a half inch of accumulation.
