EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Glacial Dam releases caused minor flooding along the Kenai River from near Seward to Skilak Lake.  Water levels reached minor flood levels, with reports of high water closing down boat access, reaching into yards, and encroaching garages that were along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall across Prince William Sound caused rapid changes in river conditions, along with widespread minor to moderate and low-land flooding, near Cordova and Valdez.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused rapid rises on the Lowe River and surrounding the Solomon Gulch Lake near Valdez, AK. Widespread lowland flooding was reported by the Floodplain Manager as waters rose rapidly during the event, rising as fast as 3 feet in under 12 hours and cresting at 15.02 ft. In doing so, the Lowe River reached moderate flood stage. The Floodplain Manager reported the Lowe River being very high but not on the road. Many low areas near the river were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Typhoon Kulap moved across northwest portions of the northern Pacific near the end of September 2022, transitioning to an extratropical low as it approached the southwestern Bering Sea.  The system was able to favorably interact and merge with a separate upper level trough over the western Bering to promote further strengthening after going extratropical, and peak sustained winds of Hurricane force were observed near the center between September 29th and 30th over the western Bering and far western Aleutians as the system reached peak intensity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strongest winds gusting to Hurricane force were observed at the Shemya/Eareckson Airforce Station ASOS during the morning of September 30th.  Communications from the observation site were interrupted during the peak of the event, but an observer was able to later confirm the peak gust recorded was 76 knots or 87 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Typhoon Merbok in the northern Pacific Ocean began approaching the Bering Sea on Thursday, September 15, 2022. This storm transitioned into an extra-tropical area of low pressure and strengthened as it traversed the Bering Sea from southwest to northeast. Impacts were first observed with high winds in the Central and Western Aleutians early on Friday, September 16. The storm continued to move northeast and peaked in intensity on Friday with a minimum central pressure of 937 mb at 4:00 AM, which is the lowest pressure ever recorded in the Bering Sea for the month of September (data since 1950). More remarkably, it appears to have been the lowest pressure recorded for any time of year along portions of its track. The storm slowly weakened but remained very powerful as it moved through the Bering Strait on Saturday morning September 17, taking a perfect track to bring significant coastal flooding to the northern Bering Sea coast. The storm continued to move northeast into the Chukchi Sea Saturday afternoon, September 17 and stalled out, gradually dissipating through Tuesday, September 20 over the Chukchi Sea.||The impacts on Friday were mainly over the Bering Sea and the Pribilof Islands in the form of high winds and seas. Buoy 46035, located at 57.016N, 177.703W in the Bering Sea recorded wind gusts to 76 mph and seas of 52 feet Friday morning. By Friday evening, and into the weekend, the low moved towards the western Alaskan coastline, bringing high winds and near record storm surge. ||What made this storm unusual was threefold: Storm strength, storm track, and the speed of its movement. As Merbok���s remnants approached the Bering Sea, an eastward transitioning disturbance reinforced the storm���s strength by infusing its energy into Merbok���s remnants, while merging cold, dry air aloft (pulled from continental east Asia), with the tropical surface air below.  This interaction increased the storm���s strength exponentially, while facilitating the storm���s extra-tropical transition. While it is common to get Hurricane-Force winds during the Fall and Winter in these extra-tropical systems, the strength of the storm was historic for the month of September. Additionally, the Bering Sea���s strongest storms typically occur when there is some sea ice present to buffer and protect the coastline from wave action. In September, there is no sea ice in the Bering or Chukchi Seas, leaving the coastline vulnerable to wind driven waves, along with the storm surge.||Also of note were the storm���s track and speed of movement. Merbok tracked from the Southwest Bering Sea in a nearly straight line into the Bering Strait at a speed that induced a ���dynamic fetch.��� This means that the storm moved at the same speed as the waves it was generating, causing winds to continue to build the same areas of high seas, thus resulting in sea heights over 50 feet. The straight line of the track to the Bering Strait meant that these huge seas, once generated, were sustained by the storm until it deposited these seas into the Western Alaska Coastline. This is what caused the record breaking storm surge at some locations.||A myriad of impacts were noted across the West Alaska Coastline. While waters rose in many villages, homes became uninhabitable or lifted off their foundations, and roads were washed out, local community leaders have stressed that many of their villages have sustained a serious loss in their way of life. Of note is the fishing fleet loss, and the many fish camps and smoke houses damaged or lost across many villages. This is more than just a physical loss of possessions and property. These camps have been in families for generations. This is a loss of food security and a place to teach traditional practices and values to the kids. ||Flood specific:|High winds and heavy rain from the remnants of Typhoon Merbok caused flooding from heavy rain and wind driven backwater along the Kuskokwim River from Bethel to the Kuskokwim Delta. Heavily impacted were the towns of Napakiak and Oscarville which are located below Bethel, the nearest river gage location. Minor flooding was also likely around the Brown Slough in Bethel. Many small villages in ungaged locations were impacted from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shemya (PASY) observation site went out about 5:00PM, so missed peak of the event as the strongest winds associated with Merbok approached the western Aleutians.  Buoy 46071, located just east of Amchitka on 09 / 15 at 5:40 pm reported 40.8G60.2kts (69.3mph).  Waves reported to 40 feet by buoy camera. Strongest winds were expected between Adak and Shemya, so likely much stronger winds were missed by observational sites in this vicinity, especially with the PASY outage.

Once again, cold temperatures in place away from the immediate coast were able to remain stubbornly entrenched over valleys from the surface to around 1000 ft above ground level as warm, moist air advected overtop of the colder air mass in place in association with the low and front approaching the northern Gulf coast.||This system was able to push a much more pronounced warm nose of air above freezing overtop of the colder air in place as precipitation moved across Prince William Sound and into the Copper River Basin on October 14th, then shifting and expanding into the Susitna Valley and Anchorage area on October 15th, with heavier precipitation finally letting up across the entire region by the morning of October 16th. The heaviest freezing rain accumulations were reported over the Copper River Basin, where up to 0.25 inches of accumulating ice was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alaska 511 (Department of Transportation) reported freezing rain and ice buildup on the Richardson Highway for the 5 AM AKST report on October 16th from MP 18 to 30 of the Richardson Highway near Thompson Pass.  A DOT provided surface observing site in Thompson Pass recorded 'unknown precipitation' for several hours through the afternoon of October 15th, with temperatures then remaining above freezing for the duration of remaining precipitation.  Ice and slush covered roads were also clearly visible on webcams along portions of the Richardson Highway through the early morning of October 16th.

Wind gusts peaked at around 74 mph at the PASN ASOS, while waves observed to 46 feet by about 7 AM Sept 16th. At 950 AM seas up to 52 feet were observed by the closest buoy.

This is a loss of food security and a place to teach traditional practices and values to the kids. ||Flood specific:|High winds and heavy rain from the remnants of Typhoon Merbok caused flooding from heavy rain and wind driven backwater along the Kuskokwim River from Bethel to the Kuskokwim Delta. Heavily impacted were the towns of Napakiak and Oscarville which are located below Bethel, the nearest river gage location. Minor flooding was also likely around the Brown Slough in Bethel. Many small villages in ungaged locations were impacted from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Adak (ADKA2) recorded a 81mph gust at 3:24 AM Sept. 16, 2022 and first hit 75mph at 5:36 PM Sept. 15, 2022 as Merbok passed to the north of the central Aleutians and pushed strong southwest winds across this portion of the Aleutian Islands.  The last 80 mph wind gust at ADKA2 occurred at 654 AM Sept 16th, and winds decreased afterwards. Several calls were made out from NWS Anchorage to Adak to determine any damage, but these attempts were unsuccessful in reaching any residents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Typhoon Merbok in the northern Pacific Ocean began approaching the Bering Sea on Thursday, September 15, 2022. This storm transitioned into an extra-tropical area of low pressure and strengthened as it traversed the Bering Sea from southwest to northeast. Impacts were first observed with high winds in the Central and Western Aleutians early on Friday, September 16. The storm continued to move northeast and peaked in intensity on Friday with a minimum central pressure of 937 mb at 4:00 AM, which is the lowest pressure ever recorded in the Bering Sea for the month of September (data since 1950). More remarkably, it appears to have been the lowest pressure recorded for any time of year along portions of its track. The storm slowly weakened but remained very powerful as it moved through the Bering Strait on Saturday morning September 17, taking a perfect track to bring significant coastal flooding to the northern Bering Sea coast. The storm continued to move northeast into the Chukchi Sea Saturday afternoon, September 17 and stalled out, gradually dissipating through Tuesday, September 20 over the Chukchi Sea.||The impacts on Friday were mainly over the Bering Sea and the Pribilof Islands in the form of high winds and seas. Buoy 46035, located at 57.016N, 177.703W in the Bering Sea recorded wind gusts to 76 mph and seas of 52 feet Friday morning. By Friday evening, and into the weekend, the low moved towards the western Alaskan coastline, bringing high winds and near record storm surge. ||What made this storm unusual was threefold: Storm strength, storm track, and the speed of its movement. As Merbok���s remnants approached the Bering Sea, an eastward transitioning disturbance reinforced the storm���s strength by infusing its energy into Merbok���s remnants, while merging cold, dry air aloft (pulled from continental east Asia), with the tropical surface air below.  This interaction increased the storm���s strength exponentially, while facilitating the storm���s extra-tropical transition. While it is common to get Hurricane-Force winds during the Fall and Winter in these extra-tropical systems, the strength of the storm was historic for the month of September. Additionally, the Bering Sea���s strongest storms typically occur when there is some sea ice present to buffer and protect the coastline from wave action. In September, there is no sea ice in the Bering or Chukchi Seas, leaving the coastline vulnerable to wind driven waves, along with the storm surge.||Also of note were the storm���s track and speed of movement. Merbok tracked from the Southwest Bering Sea in a nearly straight line into the Bering Strait at a speed that induced a ���dynamic fetch.��� This means that the storm moved at the same speed as the waves it was generating, causing winds to continue to build the same areas of high seas, thus resulting in sea heights over 50 feet. The straight line of the track to the Bering Strait meant that these huge seas, once generated, were sustained by the storm until it deposited these seas into the Western Alaska Coastline. This is what caused the record breaking storm surge at some locations.||A myriad of impacts were noted across the West Alaska Coastline. While waters rose in many villages, homes became uninhabitable or lifted off their foundations, and roads were washed out, local community leaders have stressed that many of their villages have sustained a serious loss in their way of life. Of note is the fishing fleet loss, and the many fish camps and smoke houses damaged or lost across many villages. This is more than just a physical loss of possessions and property. These camps have been in families for generations. This is a loss of food security and a place to teach traditional practices and values to the kids. ||Flood specific:|High winds and heavy rain from the remnants of Typhoon Merbok caused flooding from heavy rain and wind driven backwater along the Kuskokwim River from Bethel to the Kuskokwim Delta. Heavily impacted were the towns of Napakiak and Oscarville which are located below Bethel, the nearest river gage location. Minor flooding was also likely around the Brown Slough in Bethel. Many small villages in ungaged locations were impacted from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore and southwesterly winds moved into the Kuskokwim Delta from the evening of September 16th to the morning of September 17th as Merbok's center moved up towards Saint Lawrence Island and approached the Seward Peninsula.  At 6am AKDT September 17th, Arctic Air (tug boat) reported winds of 45 mph with gusts reaching 80 mph on the Kinak River. Toksook Bay reported the highest measured gust from ground ASOS/AWOS during the event, with a gust to 61kts at about 9 pm on September 16th.  The majority of damage observed across the Kuskokwim Delta was due to coastal flooding/erosion rather than the direct effects from the winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved over the Kuskokwim Delta on the evening of October 25th before turning southeast and moving over Bristol Bay by the morning of October 26th. As the upper-level energy of the system continued eastward, it initiated the development of a triple-point low over the northwestern Gulf of Alaska, which eventually tracked into Southeast Alaska.||Together, these systems contributed to a variety of winter weather across southern mainland Alaska, including widespread snowfall on October 25th and 26th, though some precipitation fell as rain along the coast of Bristol Bay and for a time on the southern Kenai Peninsula. The heaviest snow fell over the western Kenai Peninsula as the low over the Kuskokwim Delta pushed moist southwesterly flow up Cook Inlet while an upper-level shortwave rotated through, creating a band of heavier precipitation. The triple point low in the northern Gulf of Alaska contributed to blowing snow through Thompson Pass as a result of an enhanced pressure gradient due to its presence drawing cold air through the pass out of the Copper River Basin. Additionally, low pressure in the Gulf of Alaska combined with cold advection on the back side of the system to produce high winds through gaps in the Alaska Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted briefly to 65 knots (75 mph) out of the northwest as reported by the Sand Point ASOS during the afternoon of October 26th. Wind gusts exceeded 50 knots (58 mph) for several hours surrounding this time as part of a longer northwest gap wind event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season heavy snowfall event took place across portions of Southcentral from October 11th to 12th as a low and associate front lifted across the Gulf and interacted with colder air in place over interior parts of southern Alaska.  Deep, moist southerly flow moving into the northern Gulf coast helped set the stage for a favorable frontal overrunning event across the eastern half of Southcentral, with as much as 1 to 2 feet of new snow falling across namely the Copper River Basin by the time precipitation ended on the night of October 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple Alaska 511 (Department of Transportation) reports of Very Difficult status for highways in the Copper River Basin during and towards the end of heavy snowfall, mainly on October 12th.  A road closure was required at one point due to a multi-vehicle accident near Tolsona. A Snotel in McCarthy reported 2.2 inches SWE and 23 inches snow accumulation in the span of less than 24 hours through 11 PM AKST on October 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying extratropical low crossed the western Aleutians to enter the Bering Sea on the evening of October 27th. It tracked eastward across the southern Bering Sea, reaching the eastern Aleutians and Alaska Peninsula near peak intensity on the evening of October 28th. The low brought high winds to communities in the central and eastern Aleutians as it passed just north of the islands on October 28th. Thereafter, the low continued to draw strong winds through gaps in the Alaska Peninsula as it exited to the Gulf of Alaska, where it weakened slowly over the course of the next several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dutch Harbor AWOS reported a peak wind gust of 73 knots (84 mph) on the evening of October 28th. Wind gusts in excess of 65 knots (75 mph) were also reported by the AWOS in Akutan for a period of several hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe storm surge moved into coastal areas of the Kuskokwim Delta during the height of onshore winds and high waves battering the coast as Merbok's center moved northeast towards the Seward Peninsula on the evening of September 16th.  Some of the most severe impacts as far as coastal flooding across the Kuskokwim Delta were focused in Newtok. Patrick LeMay reported the entire community was inundated with water. Residents were cut off from the airport. They estimated 100 feet of shoreline was lost and that the riverbank eroded between 10 and 20 feet overnight. 12 homes flooded with about 35 people without a home to go back to; no homes floated off their foundation. About 60 to 70 people needed temporary shelter at the Newtok school.  A 5,000 gallon fuel tank washed away; empty fuel tanks and boats floated to the other side of the river, and a handful of boats sank. Serious river erosion also was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall across Prince William Sound caused rapid changes in river conditions, along with widespread minor to moderate and low-land flooding, near Cordova and Valdez.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains across Cordova, AK increased river levels quickly causing areas of minor flooding and lowland overflows in the area. Eyak Lake showed a rapid rise to minor flood stage, however, impacts were limited to flooding along low lying areas and along low roadways. The Eyak Lake river gage crested at 4.81 ft. Portions of the Copper River Highway, connecting Cordova to the airport, had been washed away according to the Cordova Police Department.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation began to fall across the western Kenai Peninsula on the morning of October 25th. The heaviest period of snowfall occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 25th through midnight, as indicated by ASOS reports from Kenai and Soldotna. Snow continued into the morning hours of October 26th at lighter intensity. Trained spotters reported 10.0 inches of storm total snow accumulation in Soldotna and 8.3 inches in Sterling.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alaska 511 (Department of Transportation) reported blowing and drifting snow through Thompson Pass from MP 18 to MP 30 on the morning of October 26th. A nearby DOT surface observing site recorded winds gusting to 30 knots (35 mph).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying extratropical low crossed the western Aleutians to enter the Bering Sea on the evening of October 27th. It tracked eastward across the southern Bering Sea, reaching the eastern Aleutians and Alaska Peninsula near peak intensity on the evening of October 28th. The low brought high winds to communities in the central and eastern Aleutians as it passed just north of the islands on October 28th. Thereafter, the low continued to draw strong winds through gaps in the Alaska Peninsula as it exited to the Gulf of Alaska, where it weakened slowly over the course of the next several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 65 knots (75 mph) was recorded by a NOS-NWLON observing site in Atka on the morning of October 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A suspected freeze-up jam on the Tanana River resulted in unusual wintertime flooding along the Tanana River near Harding Lake along Sewell Drive.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A suspected freeze-up ice jam on the Tanana River near Harding Lake resulted in overflow and flooding along Sewell Drive. Water inundated private property at two residences and a portion of Sewell Drive was flooded. One resident had 5 inches of water in their home. Many personal items and equipment surrounding the residences were partially submerged in the floodwaters. Water slowly receded after peaking on around December 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a six day Arctic air outflow event, a frontal band, the first of a series of systems, brought dry, fluffy, snow starting over the southern panhandle and spreading northward through the 23rd. E-ENE wind gusts of 25-40mph were widespread with reports of blowing and drifting snow. The second frontal band moved into the southern panhandle early on the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP measurement from the city of Haines was 6 inches on the morning of the 24th. Another trained spotter from town also noted 6 inches had fallen since the previous evening while three other spotters closer to Lynn Canal had to estimate the snow at 4 to 6 inches due to persistent wind and blowing snow. They noted that light dry snow had fallen all day on the 23rd, but did not start accumulating until after 6pm and ended before sunrise.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a six day Arctic air outflow event, a frontal band, the first of a series of systems, brought dry, fluffy, snow starting over the southern panhandle and spreading northward through the 23rd. E-ENE wind gusts of 25-40mph were widespread with reports of blowing and drifting snow. The second frontal band moved into the southern panhandle early on the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell at the Petersburg Airport all day on the 23rd with 3.5 inches being measured by 9pm. A lull between waves happened overnight, then picked up again around 5am on the 24th with 7.8 inches of new snow falling by 5pm, or over 12 hours.

CZ_NAME: WESTERN KUPREANOF ISLAND AND KUIU ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a six day Arctic air outflow event, a frontal band, the first of a series of systems, brought dry, fluffy, snow starting over the southern panhandle and spreading northward through the 23rd. E-ENE wind gusts of 25-40mph were widespread with reports of blowing and drifting snow. The second frontal band moved into the southern panhandle early on the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter from Port Alexander emailed estimating they had 6 inches of snow by 816am on the 23rd. Another spotter further north in the zone, in Security Bay, saw the same event in two waves of snow of 3.5 inches on the 23rd and 5.5 inches by 5pm on the 24th. That spotter reported snow had begun several hours later and had a quarter mile visibility the morning of the 23rd and a very dry consistency.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front tracking over the region from south to north brought wind and snow to much of the panhandle with greatest snowfall across Haines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports came in from Haines the day after Christmas. Spotters reported that snow began Christmas morning and had 8 inches in town by midnight. Haines Customs reported 13 inches had fallen by 7 am on the 26th and was still getting snow, meanwhile in town reports were 6-11 inches of snow that had changed to periods of freezing rain which continued for most of the day on the 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving across the Pacific brought a front with embedded shortwaves into Southeast Alaska. While precipitation remained mainly as rain in the southern panhandle, the northern panhandle saw accumulating snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 515 in the morning of the 30th, a spotter NW of Juneau reported 5 inches of snow since it began around 9pm on the 29th. Another person further up the highway at Yankee Cove estimated that 7 inches had fallen by 724am and was still falling. A COOP observer at Lena Point confirmed what others were seeing with a measurement of 6.8 inches at 920am and another 1 inch falling until mid afternoon. Snow was wet in consistency.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front moved in from the west with a well defined warm sector after a recent cold outflow event resulted in an overrunning snow event for Yakutat and portions of the northern panhandle December 11th into the 12th. Gusty winds occurred followed by a change over to moderate rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total snow of 8 inches fell at Mud Bay. The snow started around 9pm on December 11th with reports of 2.5 to 3.5 inches falling by 11am on the 12th. Then rates became moderate to heavy with another 5 inches falling over the next 6 hours with reports from the airport of 30-40 mph wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mountain wave event began ahead of an approaching frontal system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Consistent wind gust of 60-70 mph were recorded by Marine Exchange station at the the AML Dock (JMLA2) during the early morning hours of the 23rd. A peak gust of 74 mph occurred at 402am. A social media report of blown down Christmas trees was also received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across the northwest Arctic providing gusty winds, ice accumulations up to a quarter of an inch, and heavy snow at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shishmaref reported Unknown Precipitation that transitioned into rain by Thursday evening. Total precipitation was 0.04. The rain froze on cold surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across the northwest Arctic providing gusty winds, ice accumulations up to a quarter of an inch, and heavy snow at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quarter inch of freezing rain fell in Nome. Photos show over a quarter inch of ice on branches in Dexter (north of Nome).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across the northwest Arctic providing gusty winds, ice accumulations up to a quarter of an inch, and heavy snow at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An automated sensor near Kiana reported 10 inches of snow within 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across the northwest Arctic providing gusty winds, ice accumulations up to a quarter of an inch, and heavy snow at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Point Lay began reporting freezing rain early in the morning of the 1st with a mixture of rain and freezing rain continuing into morning of the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the NE Gulf of Alaska brought a front through the panhandle December 7th through the 8th. This resulted in accumulating snow, with the greatest snow totals being over the northern half of the panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total of 12.5 inches of snow fell at Haines Customs by the time snow came to an end at Haines Customs during the morning of December 8th. One inch of snow had fallen as of 7 AM AKST December 7th, with an additional 5.5 inches of snow by 130 PM, and an additional 2.5 inches by 7 PM, and the remainder falling prior to the snow letting up the following morning (December 8th). Meanwhile freezing rain was reported at the airport closer to town the morning of the 7th and likely further up the Haines Highway at the transition from rain to snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary low over the northern Gulf of Alaska wrapped a band of snow through the Yakutat area between December 20 and December 21st. The narrow nature of the band resulted in a an area of significant snowfall and wind that developed over Yakutat before the band slowly moved away from the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A retired NWS employed reported a heavy snow on the evening of the 20th with significant blowing and drifting. By 920am on the 21st it was noted that 13-15 inches of snow had fallen across town. Gusts of 20-30 mph were steady at the ASOS throughout the event with snow ending at 318 pm on the 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front moved in from the west with a well defined warm sector after a recent cold outflow event resulted in an overrunning snow event for Yakutat and portions of the northern panhandle December 11th into the 12th. Gusty winds occurred followed by a change over to moderate rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: By 646pm on the 11th a retired NWS Employee reported 2 inches of snow had fallen so far. Snow continued to fall through the night with periods of 20 to 40 mph wind gusts early in the morning of the 12th when snow changed to moderate rain between 6-8am. The WSO office estimated 10 inches had fallen later in the day, but that was after being rained on all day and was likely higher.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system northwest of Point Hope brought a front over the Chukchi Sea Coast, resulting in freezing rain,  snow gusty winds, and blowing snow from areas west of Utqiagvik.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Point Hope and Wainwright observed sustained winds near 50 mph and gusts up to 65. Cape Lisbourne had several gusts of 98 mph. This winds caused significant visibility restrictions at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low in the Gulf of Alaska deepened and moved a front over the Southeast Interior providing areas of heavy snow to the Alaska Range and from Delta Junction to Northway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northway reported 8 inches of snow in about a 24 hour timeframe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low in the Gulf of Alaska deepened and moved a front over the Southeast Interior providing areas of heavy snow to the Alaska Range and from Delta Junction to Northway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The City of Delta Junction  office estimated that  12-14 inches of snow fell in just over 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the NE Gulf of Alaska brought a front through the panhandle December 7th through the 8th. This resulted in accumulating snow, with the greatest snow totals being over the northern half of the panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling during the morning of December 7th and continued falling through the overnight period before snowfall ended during the morning of December 8th. The DOT Klondike Highway report included 9-12 inches of snow had fallen between 12 mile and White Pass. Meanwhile, near sea level, Skagway was getting snow, rain, and periods of freezing rain. The Skagway Police sent out a NIXEL message at 1054am on the 7th cautioning the public of slick roads for the Klondike Highway and Dyea road. The Skagway COOP reported freezing rain on the morning of the 8th.
